1: 'This is what you are to do to consecrate them, so they may serve me as priests: Take a young bull and two rams without defect.' The ordination ceremony requires perfect animals — the standard of tamim (without defect) that applies to all sacrificial animals and ultimately to Christ (1 Peter 1:19). The bull is for the sin offering (Exodus 29:14); the two rams are for the burnt offering (Exodus 29:18) and the ordination offering (Exodus 29:22). The full range of sacrificial categories — atonement, consecration, and total self-giving — is required to inaugurate the priesthood.
2: Unleavened bread in three forms: plain loaves, loaves mixed with oil, and wafers spread with oil. The three forms of unleavened bread accompany the ordination sacrifices, representing the purity (no leaven), the anointing (oil mixed), and the dedication (oil spread) of the priestly service. Paul's description of the new-covenant life as 'unleavened' (1 Corinthians 5:7-8) — 'the bread of sincerity and truth' — is the antitype: the whole life of the new-covenant community is to be the bread of consecration, without the leaven of the old life.
3: The bread is presented in a basket along with the bull and the two rams. The combination of grain offering and animal sacrifice — the fruit of human labor (bread) and the blood of substitutionary death (animals) — in the ordination ceremony prefigures the Lord's Supper: bread (the fruit of labor) alongside the 'blood of the covenant' (the animal-sacrifice antitype). The ordination meal of Exod 29 becomes, in the NT, the covenant meal that ordains the new-covenant community to its priestly service.
4: 'Bring Aaron and his sons to the entrance to the tent of meeting and wash them with water.' The washing at the entrance before ordination is the type of baptism. Hebrews 10:22: 'Let us draw near to God with a sincere heart and with the full assurance that faith brings, having our hearts sprinkled to cleanse us from a guilty conscience and having our bodies washed with pure water.' The priestly washing at the tent's entrance is the OT form of the new-covenant washing that inaugurates priestly service — the baptism into Christ that cleanses and consecrates.
5: 'Take the garments and dress Aaron with the tunic, the robe of the ephod, the ephod itself and the breastpiece. Fasten the ephod on him by its skillfully woven waistband.' The vestition — dressing the newly washed priest in the sacred garments — is the type of 'putting on Christ.' Galatians 3:27: 'All of you who were baptized into Christ have clothed yourselves with Christ.' The baptism (Exodus 29:4, washing) followed by the vestition (Exodus 29:5, garments) maps directly onto the NT pattern: baptism followed by the clothing of Christ's righteousness. The new believer puts on the high-priestly vestments of Christ as their ordained covering.
6: 'Put the turban on his head and attach the sacred emblem (nezer ha-kodesh) to the turban.' The nezer (consecration-crown/diadem) is the royal-priestly headpiece — the word used for the nazirite's separation (Numbers 6:7) and for the king's crown (Psalms 89:39). The turban with the golden plate combines the priestly function (covering the head for service) with the kingly marker (the crown-equivalent). Christ wears both: 'On his robe and on his thigh he has this name written: KING OF KINGS AND LORD OF LORDS' (Revelation 19:16); 'he is... priest on his throne' (Zechariah 6:13).
7: 'Take the anointing oil and anoint him by pouring it on his head.' The anointing of Aaron by oil poured over the head is the foundational act of priestly consecration — the source of the title kohen ha-mashiach (the anointed priest). Mashiach (anointed) is the root of both 'Messiah' and 'Christ' (Christos). Acts 10:38: 'God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and power.' Psalm 133 likens the Spirit's anointing to the oil poured on Aaron's head: 'It is like precious oil poured on the head... running down on Aaron's beard, down on the collar of his robe.' The Spirit poured at Jesus's baptism (Matthew 3:16) is the fulfillment of the ordination oil.
8: 'Bring his sons and dress them in tunics.' The sons receive the same vestiture as the father — derived priestly honor. In Christ, all believers receive derived priestly honor: 'To him who loves us and has freed us from our sins by his blood, and has made us to be a kingdom and priests to serve his God and Father' (Revelation 1:5-6). The sons who receive tunics from the anointed father are the type of the church that receives priestly dignity from the anointed Son.
9: 'The priesthood is theirs by a lasting ordinance (chukat olam).' The Aaronic priesthood is established as a permanent institution. Hebrews 7:23-24 makes the contrast: 'Now there have been many of those priests, since death prevented them from continuing in office; but because Jesus lives forever, he has a permanent priesthood.' The chukat olam of the Aaronic line is fulfilled and surpassed in the permanent priesthood of the Son — 'permanent' not because of hereditary succession but because 'he always lives.'
10: 'Bring the bull to the front of the tent of meeting, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on its head.' The laying on of hands (samach) identifies the offerer with the animal — the guilt of the one is transferred to the other. Paul in 2 Corinthians 5:21: 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us.' The ordination sin offering is the type: the priestly hands lay their guilt on the head of the substitute; at the cross, the Father makes the Son to be sin — the ultimate hand-laying of transferred guilt.
11: 'Slaughter it in the LORD's presence at the entrance to the tent of meeting.' The sin offering is killed before YHWH — in the divine presence, not concealed. The crucifixion is similarly public: 'Jesus Christ was clearly portrayed as crucified' (Galatians 3:1). The atoning death happens in the open, before God and the watching world, not in a private transaction. The public nature of the ordination sin offering anticipates the 'open demonstration' of the cross (Romans 3:25-26).
12: Some of the bull's blood put on the horns of the altar, the rest poured at the base. The blood applied to the altar's horns consecrates the instrument of sacrifice; the blood poured at the base grounds the atonement in the earth. Hebrews 9:21-22: Moses 'sprinkled with the blood both the tabernacle and everything used in its ceremonies... without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness.' The altar's blood-anointing is the type of the consecration that Christ's blood accomplishes — he 'through the eternal Spirit offered himself unblemished to God' and thereby 'cleanse[s] our consciences... so that we may serve the living God' (Hebrews 9:14).
13: The fat of the internal organs, liver lobe, and kidneys — the richest portions — burned on the altar. The fat is YHWH's portion in every sacrifice (Leviticus 3:16: 'all the fat is the LORD's'). The giving of the best, the richest, the most vital portion to God is the type of the total consecration that the gospel demands: 'I urge you, brothers and sisters, in view of God's mercy, to offer your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and pleasing to God — this is your true and proper worship' (Romans 12:1).
14: 'But burn the bull's flesh and its hide and its intestines outside the camp. It is a sin offering.' The sin offering is burned outside the camp — not at the altar but removed from the sacred precincts, bearing the defilement away from the holy space. Hebrews 13:11-13: 'The high priest carries the blood of animals into the most holy place as a sin offering, but the bodies are burned outside the camp. And so Jesus also suffered outside the city gate to make the people holy through his own blood. Let us, then, go to him outside the camp, bearing the disgrace he bore.' The site of Jesus's crucifixion — outside Jerusalem's walls — is the spatial fulfillment of the sin-offering's burning outside the camp.
15: 'Take one of the rams, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on its head.' The second sacrifice is the burnt offering — the ram wholly consumed. The laying of hands transfers the worshippers' consecration to the animal, which gives its whole life to God. Romans 12:1 calls for 'living sacrifices' — the new-covenant equivalents of the burnt offering that give the whole self to God. But the ground of this total consecration is Christ's own total self-offering, which the burnt offering anticipates.
16: Slaughter the ram and splash its blood against the sides of the altar. The blood splashed on the altar's sides is the blood of consecration — not the blood of atonement (like the sin offering) but the blood of dedication. Ephesians 5:2: 'Live a life of love, just as Christ loved us and gave himself up for us as a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God.' The burnt offering's blood of dedication points to Christ's self-offering as an act of love and total consecration to the Father's will.
17: The ram is cut into pieces, the internal organs and legs washed, then reassembled with the head. The detailed cutting and washing of the sacrifice ensures its purity — nothing unclean is burned on the altar. The concern for the integrity of the sacrifice points to the requirement of Christ's perfection as the sacrifice: 'a lamb without blemish or defect' (1 Peter 1:19). The washing and inspection of the sacrifice is the OT form of the NT declaration that the sacrifice must be 'perfect' to accomplish atonement.
18: 'Then burn the entire ram on the altar. It is a burnt offering to the LORD, a food offering (isheh), an aroma pleasing to the LORD (re'ach nicho'ach laYHWH).' The totally consumed burnt offering produces the 'aroma pleasing to YHWH' — the divine acceptance of the total self-giving. Ephesians 5:2 applies this formula directly to Christ: 'Christ loved us and gave himself up for us as a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' — using precisely the LXX language of osme euodias (sweet aroma) that translates re'ach nicho'ach. Christ's sacrifice is the burnt offering that satisfies completely.
19: 'Take the other ram, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on its head.' The third animal — the ordination ram (eil hammilu'im, the ram of filling) — is the distinctive sacrifice of the ordination ceremony. The laying of hands on the ordination ram expresses not primarily guilt-transfer but identification: the priests are identified with the sacrifice that initiates their ministry. Jesus identifies himself with the sacrifice that initiates his priestly ministry: 'For their sakes I consecrate myself' (John 17:19) — the Gethsemane consecration that leads to the cross.
20: 'Slaughter it, take some of its blood and put it on the lobes of the right ears of Aaron and his sons, on the thumbs of their right hands and on the big toes of their right feet.' The blood applied to ear, thumb, and big toe — the whole person consecrated from hearing (ear) to action (thumb/hand) to walking (toe/foot). Hebrews 10:5-7 (citing Ps 40) applies this total-person consecration to Christ: 'A body you prepared for me... I have come to do your will, my God' — the ear that hears the Father's will, the hands that do the priestly work, the feet that walk the path of obedience even to death.
21: 'And take some of the blood on the altar and some of the anointing oil and sprinkle it on Aaron and his garments and on his sons and their garments.' The combination of blood and anointing oil on the high priest — the sacrifice (blood) and the Spirit (oil) together consecrate him for ministry. This combination points to Christ: the one anointed by the Spirit at baptism (the oil) who then offered himself in blood at the cross. The two elements — Spirit and blood — are the twin qualifications of the eternal high priest (1 John 5:6-8 cites these as witnesses).
22: The fat, fat tail, fat around the organs, liver lobe, kidneys, and the right thigh are taken from the ordination ram — the choicest portions. The right thigh (the wave offering portion) is the priest's share in subsequent peace offerings. The priest participates in the very sacrifice he offers — he eats from what he waves before God. Jesus both offers the sacrifice and participates in its benefits: 'for their sakes I consecrate myself, that they too may be truly consecrated' (John 17:19). The priest who offers is also the one through whom others are fed (John 6:51).
23: From the basket of unleavened bread — a loaf, a thick loaf with oil, and a wafer — taken together with the ordination ram portions. The combination of bread and meat in the wave offering anticipates the Lord's Supper's combination of bread and cup. The ordination meal that consecrates the Aaronic priests for their ministry becomes the Lord's Supper that constitutes the new-covenant community as 'a royal priesthood' (1 Peter 2:9) consecrated for their ministry of worship and witness.
24: 'Put all these in the hands of Aaron and his sons and have them wave them before the LORD as a wave offering.' The wave offering (t'nufah) — presented before YHWH with a back-and-forth motion — represents presenting the offering to God and receiving it back. Jesus presents himself to the Father in the cross and is received back in the resurrection. Hebrews 9:11-12: he 'went through the greater and more perfect tabernacle... and... entered the most holy place once for all by his own blood' — the ultimate wave offering, presented and received.
25: 'Then take them from their hands and burn them on the altar along with the burnt offering for a pleasing aroma to the LORD.' The ordination portions are burned on top of the burnt offering — the ordination-consecration burns with the total-consecration. The two fires merge. In Christ, the total self-giving (burnt offering) and the consecration of ministry (ordination offering) are one act: the cross is simultaneously the total offering to God and the inauguration of the eternal high-priestly ministry. The two fires merge into one altar.
26: 'After you take the breast of the ordination ram belonging to Aaron, wave it before the LORD as a wave offering; it will be your share.' The breast — the portion of the heart — belongs to Moses (the one performing the ordination) as his share. The priestly portions are distributed: heart to the ordaining leader, thigh to the ordained. The ordination creates a sharing community where each participant receives a portion of the sacrifice. The Lord's Supper creates the same distributed community: 'Is not the cup of thanksgiving a participation in the blood of Christ? And is not the bread that we break a participation in the body of Christ?' (1 Corinthians 10:16).
27: 'Consecrate those parts of the ordination ram that belong to Aaron and his sons: the breast that was waved and the thigh that was presented.' The wave breast and the lifted thigh are the portions that belong to the priesthood in all future peace offerings. The priestly share of the sacrifice — the ongoing provision that sustains the priests in their service — is the type of Christ's ongoing provision for the church through the Eucharist. The priest is fed by the very sacrifice he offers; the church is nourished by the body and blood of the one it commemorates.
28: 'This is always to be the regular share from the Israelites for Aaron and his sons. It is the contribution the Israelites are to make to the LORD from their fellowship offerings.' The priestly share is constitutionally established — not a favor but a right enshrined in the covenant. 1 Corinthians 9:14: 'The Lord has commanded that those who preach the gospel should receive their living from the gospel.' The NT establishes the pattern of the Levitical priesthood for the support of gospel ministers, grounded in the same principle: those who serve at the altar share in what is offered.
29: 'Aaron's sacred garments will belong to his descendants after him, so that they can be anointed and ordained in them.' The vestments pass from father to son — the priesthood is inherited through succession. Hebrews 7:23: 'Now there have been many of those priests, since death prevented them from continuing in office.' The inheritance of vestments by each successive high priest encodes the problem: mortality requires succession, which means the priesthood is never fully permanent. Only a priest who never dies can hold the office permanently — and that priest is the 'Son who has been made perfect for ever' (Hebrews 7:28).
30: 'The son who succeeds him as priest and comes to the tent of meeting to minister in the holy place is to wear them seven days.' The seven-day re-ordination with the inherited vestments. Seven days = the complete cycle, the full period. The new high priest serves a week in the vestments before beginning independent ministry. Christ's priestly ministry is inaugurated not by seven days but by the once-for-all 'one sacrifice for sins for all time' (Hebrews 10:12) after which he 'sat down at the right hand of God.' The period of ordination gives way to the enthronement of the eternal priest.
31: 'Take the ram of the ordination and cook the meat in a sacred place.' The ordination ram's meat is cooked in the holy precinct — the priestly meal is inseparable from the holy space. The Lord's Supper is similarly tied to sacred context: 'When you come together as a church' (1 Corinthians 11:18); 'Is not the cup of thanksgiving for which we give thanks a participation in the blood of Christ?' (1 Corinthians 10:16). The covenant meal belongs to the covenant assembly gathered in Christ's name.
32: 'At the entrance to the tent of meeting, Aaron and his sons are to eat the meat of the ram and the bread that is in the basket.' The ordination meal at the tent's entrance — the threshold of sacred space — is the priestly community's inaugural feast. The Last Supper is the new-covenant ordination meal: Jesus gives the disciples the bread and cup 'before he suffered' (Luke 22:15), inaugurating their priestly-apostolic ministry with the covenant meal. The entrance to the tent of meeting becomes the upper room; the ordination feast becomes the Eucharistic foundation.
33: 'They are to eat these offerings by which atonement was made for their ordination and consecration. But no one else may eat them, because they are sacred.' The atonement-meal is restricted to those for whom atonement was made — the ordained. The Lord's Supper similarly presupposes covenant membership (1 Corinthians 11:27-29): the Eucharistic bread and cup are the sacred food of those who have been consecrated through baptism and faith. The exclusivity of the ordination meal is not elitism but the appropriate restriction of sacred food to those for whom it was offered.
34: 'If any of the meat of the ordination ram or any bread is left over till morning, burn it up. It must not be eaten, because it is sacred.' Nothing of the sacred ordination meal may be profaned by carrying it into ordinary time. The total consumption that cannot be deferred mirrors Christ's 'it is finished' (John 19:30) — the sacrifice is complete, accomplished, not to be continued or supplemented. The burning of leftovers is the ritual enactment of the once-for-all completeness of the atonement.
35: 'Do for Aaron and his sons everything I have commanded you, taking seven days to ordain them.' The complete seven-day ordination — the full cycle of consecration. The number seven signals completeness throughout Scripture (seven days of creation, seven of Passover, seven of Unleavened Bread). Christ's ordination was not seven days but his entire thirty-three-year life — the full cycle of human existence lived in perfect obedience — culminating in the cross, after which he is 'designated by God to be high priest in the order of Melchizedek' (Hebrews 5:10).
36: 'Sacrifice a bull each day as a sin offering to make atonement. Purify the altar by making atonement for it, and anoint it to consecrate it.' Even the altar requires seven days of atonement-sacrifices before it can function as the locus of sacrifice. The instrument of atonement itself needs atonement — the altar is holy but imperfect, made by human hands. Christ's altar is his own body (Hebrews 13:10); it requires no purification because 'through the eternal Spirit he offered himself unblemished to God' (Hebrews 9:14). The altar that needs no seven-day consecration is the perfect Mediator himself.
37: 'For seven days make atonement for the altar and consecrate it. Then the altar will be most holy, and whatever touches it will be holy.' The altar becomes the source of holiness — whatever touches it is consecrated. Jesus reverses the normal direction: when the unclean touch him, he does not become unclean but they become clean (the leper, Matthew 8:3; the bleeding woman, Matthew 9:20-22). The consecrated altar of the OT communicates holiness by contact; Christ the living altar communicates holiness by faith-contact to all who come to him.
38: 'This is what you are to offer on the altar regularly each day: two lambs a year old.' The tamid — the daily regular offering — two lambs, morning and evening. The tamid structures Israel's worship day: it begins and ends with the lamb. Hebrews 7:27 makes the contrast explicit: 'Unlike the other high priests, he does not need to offer sacrifices day after day, first for his own sins, and then for the sins of the people. He sacrificed for their sins once for all when he offered himself.' The daily two-lamb tamid is what the Aaronic priesthood requires; Christ's once-for-all self-offering replaces the daily lambs.
39: 'Offer one in the morning and the other at twilight.' The day is bookended by the tamid lambs — the day begins and ends under sacrifice, under the covering of atonement. The Christian's day begins and ends under the cross — 'forgive us our sins, as we also forgive' in the morning (Matthew 6:12) and 'the evening sacrifice' of praise and confession (Psalms 141:2). But the ground of these daily prayers is not a daily lamb but the once-for-all cross that covers every morning and every evening.
40: With the morning lamb: a grain offering of finest flour with olive oil and a drink offering of wine. The grain, oil, and wine that accompany the lamb constitute the full range of agricultural bounty — the whole harvest in service of the sacrifice. The Lord's Supper uses bread (grain) and wine (drink offering) as the memorial elements — the same agricultural offerings that accompanied the tamid lamb, now accompanying the once-for-all Lamb in the memorial that the church maintains until he comes.
41: 'Sacrifice the other lamb at twilight with the same grain offering and drink offering... This is a food offering, an aroma pleasing to the LORD.' The evening sacrifice closes the day with the same elements as the morning — the consistency of the covenant relationship. Paul in 1 Thessalonians 5:17: 'pray continually.' The bookending of the day with sacrifice (tamid) is the OT form of the NT's unceasing prayer — the life of the covenant community is lived under the covering of the priestly offering from first light to last.
42: 'For the generations to come this burnt offering is to be made regularly at the entrance to the tent of meeting, before the LORD. There I will meet with you and speak to you.' The tent of meeting (ohel mo'ed — 'tent of appointed meeting') is the place of the divine-human encounter structurally incorporated into Israel's life through the tamid. Jesus is the final meeting-place — 'I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me' (John 14:6). The tent where YHWH meets with Israel is fulfilled in the person of the Son who is himself the locus of the divine-human encounter.
43: 'There also I will meet with the Israelites, and the place will be consecrated by my glory.' The divine presence consecrates the meeting place — not the architecture but the divine glory (kavod) makes the tent holy. John 1:14: 'The Word became flesh and made his dwelling (eskenos — pitched his tent) among us. We have seen his glory (doxa), the glory of the one and only Son.' The tabernacle's glory is the type; the incarnate Son's glory is the antitype. Where Christ is, the place is consecrated — not a building but a person.
44: 'So I will consecrate the tent of meeting and the altar and will consecrate Aaron and his sons to serve me as priests.' The consecration flows from divine initiative: YHWH consecrates, not human effort. John 17:17-19: 'Sanctify them by the truth; your word is truth... For them I sanctify myself, that they too may be truly sanctified.' The divine consecration of the priests (Exodus 29:44) is the type of Christ's self-consecration through which the new-covenant priests are sanctified. The source of all holiness is the same divine initiative.
45: 'Then I will dwell among the Israelites and be their God.' The covenant formula reached its OT climax: divine dwelling with the covenant people as their God. John 1:14: 'the Word became flesh and dwelt (eskenosen — tabernacled) among us.' The incarnation is the fulfillment of Exodus 29:45 in a mode more direct than the tabernacle could achieve — not a cloud of glory in a tent but the Son of God in human flesh, walking among his people as their Emmanuel ('God with us,' Matthew 1:23). The goal of the entire tabernacle system is achieved personally in the Incarnation.
46: 'They will know that I am the LORD their God, who brought them out of Egypt so that I might dwell among them.' The goal of the Exodus is stated: not merely liberation from slavery but divine dwelling with humanity. The entire Exodus — the plagues, the Passover, the sea-crossing, the Sinai covenant, the tabernacle instructions — moves toward this single purpose: God dwelling with his people. The NT fulfillment moves through three stages: the incarnation (John 1:14), the indwelling Spirit (John 14:17; 1 Corinthians 3:16), and the final consummation (Revelation 21:3: 'God's dwelling place is now among the people'). The 'I will dwell among them' of Exodus 29:46 is the great promise that drives the entire biblical narrative to its conclusion in the new creation.
